medications.indication_rationale: First-line topical anti-inflammatory for painful recurrent aphthous ulceration — reduces inflammation, pain and episode duration. Applied as a thin protective film over each ulcer; not rubbed in.
medications.dose_route_frequency: apply a thin layer to each ulcer 2 to 3 times daily after meals and at bedtime (per the source note)
contraindications_in_this_case: Avoid if a herpetic or fungal oral infection is suspected (topical steroid can worsen infection) — excluded here on history
Prolonged use risks secondary oral candidiasis — limit to the acute episode

medications.drug_name: topical oral local anaesthetic (e.g. lidocaine gel / benzydamine mouth rinse)
medications.indication_rationale: Short-acting topical analgesia applied to the ulcers before drinking allows the patient to resume oral fluid intake, directly addressing the dehydration risk. In the source note the clinician applied 2% viscous lidocaine to the ulcer beds; the telehealth-appropriate equivalent is a patient-applied topical anaesthetic.
medications.dose_route_frequency: apply a small amount of topical anaesthetic to the ulcers shortly before drinking or eating, per product directions (source note: topical 2% viscous lidocaine to the ulcer beds)
contraindications_in_this_case: Do not over-apply — excessive pharyngeal anaesthesia can impair the swallow/gag reflex and risk aspiration

medications.indication_rationale: Provides background analgesia, though the patient reports it gives little relief of the topical mucosal pain — hence topical measures are the mainstay. Included because it is a reasonable adjunct.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 1000 mg orally every 6 hours as needed, maximum 4 g in 24 hours (per the source note)

medications.indication_rationale: Aphthous ulceration is not an infection; there is no vesicular prodrome, keratinised mucosa is spared, and there are no systemic features. Prescribing an antiviral would be an error of commission based on misclassifying the ulcers as herpetic.
There is no bacterial infection. Systemic antibiotics have no role in aphthous ulceration and represent inappropriate prescribing.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Drink through a wide-bore straw placed past the front teeth so fluid bypasses the ulcers on the lips and front cheeks, allowing a full glass of water without triggering the pain.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Mechanically separates fluid from the ulcerated mucosa, enabling hydration despite the pain — the direct fix for the functional problem.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Temporarily avoid acidic (citrus, tomato), heavily spiced, and rough/salty foods (e.g. crisps) that chemically or mechanically irritate the ulcers; favour bland, soft, cool foods and fluids.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reduces pain and avoids stripping the healing mucosal layer.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Recognise the stress-linked recurrence pattern and use stress-reduction strategies; maintain good oral hygiene with a gentle technique.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Stress is the recurring trigger; addressing it may reduce future episode frequency.

patient_education_points.point: These are mouth ulcers caused by a localised inflammatory reaction, not an infection — they are not contagious and they heal on their own, usually within 7 to 14 days without scarring.
Dab the steroid paste gently over each ulcer and let it form a protective film — do not rub it in.
The priority right now is to get you drinking again: use the topical numbing product and the straw technique so you can keep your fluids up.
See a doctor if any single ulcer has not healed after 3 weeks — a long-lasting ulcer needs to be examined to rule out anything more serious.

follow_up_plan.interval: Review with your regular GP in 7 to 10 days if the ulcers have not significantly healed; arrange a routine haematinic screen (iron, B12, folate) for the recurrent pattern
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Ulcer healing, ability to maintain hydration and nutrition, recurrence frequency, and whether a haematinic deficiency is contributing
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Unable to keep fluids down despite topical analgesia, signs of dehydration (little/no urine, dizziness on standing), ulcers spreading or new fever with swollen neck glands, or any ulcer lasting beyond 3 weeks

must_include_items: Recognise a benign, self-limiting recurrent aphthous ulceration from the history and video-observable sores
Advise topical corticosteroid (dental paste) and topical analgesia to control pain and enable oral intake
Give practical hydration advice (topical anaesthetic before drinking + straw technique)
Provide a conservative dehydration safety-net and the 3-week non-healing-ulcer review advice
Keep triage LOW (telehealth/GP-manageable) — do not escalate a benign presentation to urgent in-person care or ED
acceptable_alternatives: An over-the-counter topical analgesic (e.g. benzydamine mouth rinse or a protective/anaesthetic oral gel) in place of the specific agents, if the steroid paste is not accessible
Antiseptic mouthwash (e.g. chlorhexidine) as an adjunct
errors_of_omission: Failing to address the inability to drink / dehydration risk
Failing to safety-net the 3-week non-healing ulcer rule
Failing to give any symptomatic treatment advice
errors_of_commission: Over-triaging the benign ulcers to ED, 000, or urgent same-day in-person review in the absence of established dehydration
Prescribing a systemic antibiotic or an oral antiviral
Misclassifying the ulcers as herpes simplex
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass requires recognising the condition as benign and self-limiting, giving symptomatic topical management that enables the patient to rehydrate, and assigning an appropriately LOW triage tier with a dehydration safety-net. Over-triaging a canker-sore exacerbation to urgent in-person care is a defect, not a safe default.
